text: The surgeon conducts an initial survey of the abdominal cavity, checking for any extra colonic disease or metastasis in the peritoneum or solid organs. The sigmoid mesentery is then lifted for further examination.

The surgeon lifts the sigmoid mesentery to identify the vascular pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery. Dissection begins just below the arterial pedicle, carefully avoiding the bifurcation and pre-sacral vessels to prevent nerve injury. The avascular plane is visible, with no named vessels traversing it, allowing for easy dissection using a harmonic device.

The surgeon identifies the ureters, which are pushed posteriorly, and gently dissects them down. The dissection plane is developed from medial to lateral, incising the lateral pelvic attachments of the sigmoid colon and upper rectum along the line of Toldt, leaving the fascia intact and carrying the dissection close to the mesentery.

The surgeon carefully preserves the gonadal vessels and ureter while dissecting around the external iliac arteries and vein. The posterior plane is dissected from medial to lateral, and once opened, attention shifts to isolating the inferior mesenteric pedicle.

The surgeon retracts the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery, leaving a 1.5 cm stump. Fibro-fatty tissues surrounding the artery are dissected away, isolating the artery posteriorly. Care is taken to preserve nearby nerves during the dissection.

The surgeon circumferentially isolates the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle, applying hemolog clips to ligate the vessel. Metal clips are used to reinforce the ligation before the pedicle is transected.

The surgeon ligates the pedicle, increasing the mobility of the colon, and then transects it using sharp scissors. The descending colon is mobilized towards the elevator muscle for a low anastomosis. The branch of the inferior mesenteric vein is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by further lateral dissection.
The surgeon applies medium-large titanium clips to the inferior mesenteric vein for its division. Following this, the focus shifts towards the pelvis to initiate the total mesorectal excision.

The surgeon lifts the rectum anteriorly, using traction and counter-traction to facilitate dissection. The lateral attachments of the peritoneum are incised, and dissection proceeds in the plane between the visceral fascia and the endopelvic fascia. Nerves are pushed down, lying posteriorly within the pre-sacral fascia. The mesorectum, containing fat, lymph nodes, and vessels, is dissected between the visceral and parietal layers. As the dissection progresses, the Valdez fissure is encountered and sharply incised to reach the brim of the levator ani muscle.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, carefully maintaining the dissection plane between the visceral and parietal layers to avoid entering the mesorectum or pre-sacral area, which could cause bleeding.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection of the rectum, starting posteriorly, then moving laterally, and finally anteriorly. As the mid rectum is crossed, the muscular rectal tube is visibly coned in the lower rectum. The lateral attachments are incised, and the anterior incision is extended just below the bladder.

The surgeon enters the Denonvilliers fascia, dissecting anteriorly between its layers. Laterally, the lateral rectal stalks containing the middle rectal vessels are addressed using harmonic devices. The dissection proceeds within the loose areolar plane of the rectum, completing the circumferential dissection.

The surgeon uses an Ethicon Endopath powered 60 mm green stapler to clamp below the rectum, ensuring proper placement. If the tumor is lower, a small fist is used to push in the anal canal for effective clamping. An additional load of the stapler is required to cut the rectal tube, taking care to avoid entangling tissue within the staple line.

The surgeon ensures a 360-degree view around the tip and shaft of the stapler before firing the second load. After firing, a small bridge of tissue may remain, which can be cut using additional instruments.

The surgeon completes the firing of the stapler, with visible pins on the lateral side. A small finesse incision is made to bring out the entire specimen, and the mesentery is divided. The distal end of the proximal colon is exposed, and a purse string suture is made using two-zero Prolene. The anvil of the CDH stapler is inserted into the colon, and the purse string is tied around the anvil.

The surgeon ensures the colon is taut before bringing the proximal limb inside. The wound is closed using an ALICE stapler. Pneumoperitoneum is recreated, and the head of the circular stapler is introduced transrectally, puncturing through the distal rectal stump. The anvil is aligned with the head of the stapler.

The surgeon lifts the bladder, seminal vesicles, and prostate anteriorly, ensuring that only bowel-to-bowel approximation occurs without entangling other tissues. The Ethicon 29 mm stapler is fired, cutting the tissue in a single go. The proximal purse string used to tie the proximal loop is freed and removed, completing the anastomosis before loosening the stapler.

The surgeon gently removes the stapler by tilting it upward, downward, and laterally, ensuring no undue traction on the anastomotic suture line. Air is inflated into the colon to check for leaks, showing distension without any air leak in the pelvic cavity. Saline is then sucked out, and hemostasis is secured, with no drain placed for drainage.
